toolbar.hint: Click any person to inspect · Yellow ring = You

environment.footer_note: Environmental stressors shift individual CFS risk in real time. Higher economic stress and pollution increase post-viral CFS rates; better healthcare access and social infrastructure reduce them.

detail.click_hint: Click any person in the simulation to see their individual profile, risk factors, and — if they develop ME/CFS — a brainstem disruption diagram.

generic.exposed: {name} was recently exposed. Symptoms may appear within days.
generic.infectious: {name} is in the acute phase and currently infectious.
generic.recovering: {name} is past the acute phase. Long-term outcome is still forming.
generic.long_covid: {name} has persistent post-viral symptoms but does not meet ME/CFS criteria.
generic.cfs: {name} has developed ME/CFS. Fatigue, PEM, and cognitive symptoms are limiting daily function.
generic.severe_cfs: {name} has severe ME/CFS. {name} is largely or fully bedbound.

viewpoint.healthy: You haven't been infected yet. Your risk factors are visible below — stay aware.
viewpoint.exposed: You've been exposed. You may not feel it yet, but the virus has entered your system.
viewpoint.infectious: You're in the acute phase. You're contagious. Rest, fluids, and medical monitoring matter now.
viewpoint.recovering: The acute phase has passed. Your body is still processing. How this resolves will be determined over the coming months.
viewpoint.recovered: You recovered fully. Your risk factors were present, but the outcome went in your favour.
viewpoint.long_covid: You have persistent post-viral symptoms — fatigue, cognitive difficulty. Not ME/CFS, but not gone either.
viewpoint.cfs: You've developed ME/CFS. This is your new reality. The brainstem disruption model below shows what's happening in your nervous system.
viewpoint.severe_cfs: You have severe ME/CFS. You are largely bedbound. The gap between your cognitive self and your physical capacity is profound.

sections.heading: What this simulates
sections.body: A viral outbreak moves through a community of 100 individuals. Each person's probability of developing ME/CFS post-infection is computed from a multi-factor risk model drawn from published research.

sections.heading: ME/CFS risk factors modelled
list: Prior EBV seropositive status (~90% of adults)
Immune function at time of infection

sections.heading: Brainstem disruption model
sections.body: For individuals who develop ME/CFS, the profile view shows a simplified diagram of the brainstem and autonomic nervous system. The model reflects current understanding of dorsal vagal complex hyperactivation, HPA axis blunting, and vagal dysregulation in ME/CFS.
sections.detail: Key structures shown: Hypothalamus / HPA axis, Nucleus Tractus Solitarius (NTS), Dorsal Vagal Complex (DVC), Locus Coeruleus (LC), Vagus nerve.

sections.heading: Base rates used
sections.body: ~12% post-viral ME/CFS incidence after severe viral illness (meta-analysis midpoint). Adjusted multiplicatively by individual and environmental risk factors.

sections.heading: References
sections.body: Wessely et al. 1995; Jason et al. 2006; Tomas & Newton 2018; Porges polyvagal theory; VanElzakker vagus nerve hypothesis; Naviaux metabolic model.

sections.heading: This is a model, not a prediction
sections.body: All values are illustrative. Individual outcomes are stochastic. The simulation is designed to make the systemic nature of ME/CFS tangible, not to provide clinical guidance.
